Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of just study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Sometimes I'd go days without opening my notes, then cram a bunch of listening to lecture recordings in one sitting. My schedule was pretty chaotic between my extracurriculars and just life stuff, so organizing study time wasn't my strong suit.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than anything else. If I had an assignment coming up, I'd suddenly become super focused for a day or two. But during quieter weeks, I honestly just drifted and didn't prioritize studying at all. Regular study times felt impossible with everything else going on.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or realizing I had no idea what was happening in class. Sometimes my classmates would mention something in our group chat and I'd panic-log in to check what I missed. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to feeling behind or confused about something.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I logged in more consistently, but it never stuck as a routine. I'd get distracted by other things or just not feel motivated enough to make it a regular thing. The LMS always felt like something I went to when I had to, not because I wanted to.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other stuff or just lie down and absorb the information. The discussion boards were okay too because I could see what other students were confused about and realize I wasn't alone. Those felt more helpful than reading dense PDFs.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They just felt overwhelming, and with my limited resources, printing stuff wasn't really an option. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate payoff, like it counting toward my grade or helping me understand something I was stuck on. If it felt abstract or like busy work, I just couldn't bring myself to care. I needed to feel like the effort would actually matter for something concrete.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. If it wasn't required, I probably didn't do it. Required activities had this weight to them because of grades, but optional ones felt like suggestions I could ignore. With limited time and energy, I had to pick my battles, and optional activities lost every time.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't at my computer. I also had some notes from classmates that I'd study from instead of going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd just think through concepts while commuting or doing chores, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures or discussing concepts with friends over text, but none of that would register on the system. I also did a lot of mental review, like just thinking through problems in my head during my extracurricular activities. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual learning happening in my brain.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd download a lecture once and then replay it multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with some of the slides - I'd save them to my phone and review them whenever. Going back to the LMS repeatedly felt unnecessary when I already had what I needed saved locally.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, and the course material started getting more complex. I felt this weird disconnect where I knew I should care more, but I just couldn't summon the energy. The mindfulness stuff they introduced helped a bit with the stress, but it didn't really fix my engagement problem.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Competing responsibilities from my activities outside class always seemed more immediate and engaging. Sometimes I'd be so mentally drained that even logging in felt like too much effort. My low resources situation also meant I was often dealing with other life stuff that took priority.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd finish a reading and realize I absorbed nothing. It was like going through the motions without actually being present, just checking boxes mentally without any real learning happening.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but convince myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would become next week. Part of it was just not feeling connected to the material, and part was that other things felt more pressing. Procrastination was basically my default mode this semester.
Q16: Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me to actually do stuff. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. The LMS probably shows these weird spikes in my usage pattern. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have engaged at all.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did make me think about trying harder next time. But honestly, by the time feedback came, I'd already mentally moved on to the next crisis. It didn't really change my patterns much because I was always in reactive mode rather than planning ahead.
Q18: The structure felt kind of loose, which was both good and bad. Good because I didn't feel suffocated, bad because I needed more structure to stay on track. When things were spread out, I'd lose track of what was happening. A tighter, more predictable structure might have helped me stay more engaged.
Q19: Having audio options made things easier for me since that's how I learn best. But the sheer amount of reading materials was discouraging - I'd open a module and see walls of text and just close it. More bite-sized content or audio alternatives would have helped me stay engaged instead of feeling overwhelmed.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure out what they need to do or searching for something specific without actually completing work. Or they might be anxious about the course and checking in a lot without being able to focus enough to submit anything. Activity doesn't always mean productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline and did okay on exams because I could listen to recordings repeatedly. The LMS activity doesn't capture all the learning that happens outside the platform. Some of us just work differently.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and how long pages were open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do away from the screen. It's like measuring only the visible tip of an iceberg.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when the material connects to something meaningful for you. I wasn't really engaged this semester - I was just surviving and getting by, which feels different.
Q24: The data wouldn't show that I was actually pretty happy this semester despite my low engagement - my extracurriculars and social life were going well. It also wouldn't show how much I learned through listening to recordings offline or talking with friends. My engagement with the course was low, but my engagement with life was fine, and those are different things.
